upsc_weightage.section: GS Paper I - Indian and World Geography

ncert.subject: Practical Work in Geography Part I
ncert.focus: Practical diagrams, map skills, field observations

revision_tasks.day_1: Revise all diagrams of Earth's layers and seismic waves
revision_tasks.day_2: Plate boundaries - draw and label convergent, divergent, transform
revision_tasks.day_3: Full mock test - Earth's interior and plate tectonics

key_concepts: Crust - continental crust (30-70 km), oceanic crust (5-7 km), composition (silicates, granite, basalt)
Mohorovicic Discontinuity (Moho) - boundary between crust and mantle at 35-70 km depth
Mantle - two layers: upper mantle (includes lithosphere and asthenosphere), lower mantle
Asthenosphere - zone of partial melting, plasticity allows plate movement
Outer core - liquid layer, predominantly iron and nickel, generates magnetic field
Inner core - solid layer due to immense pressure, temperature ~5,200°C
Temperature gradient - increases approximately 25-30°C per kilometer
Isostasy - concept of vertical equilibrium of crust, explains mountain elevation
Density variations - crust: 2.7-3.0 g/cm³, mantle: 3.3-5.5 g/cm³, core: 9-13 g/cm³
Mantle convection - drives plate movements, hot material rises, cool descends
Core composition evidence - meteorite analysis, seismic data, magnetic measurements
Geothermal gradient and Earth's internal heat source - radioactive elements (U, Th, K)
Pressure increase with depth and its effect on rock properties
Joules heating in the core and its role in maintaining Earth's magnetic field
Recent discoveries - inner core rotation, possible 5th layer
important_diagrams: Earth's internal layers (Crust, Mantle, Outer Core, Inner Core) - vertical cross-section
Mohorovicic Discontinuity and other discontinuities - labeled diagram
Temperature and pressure gradient graph with depth
Density variation diagram showing discontinuities
Composition of each layer - table format with percentages
Mantle convection cells - showing rising and sinking currents
Isostatic equilibrium - mountain vs oceanic crust comparison
topics.map_work: Not applicable for interior structure

topics.topic_name: Seismic Waves & Evidence of Earth's Interior
key_concepts: P-waves (Primary/Compressional) - fastest, travel through solids and liquids, speed 5.5-8.5 km/s
S-waves (Secondary/Shear) - slower, travel only through solids, speed 3.2-7.3 km/s
L-waves (Longitudinal/Surface) - slowest, cause maximum damage, confined to surface
Seismic shadow zones - P-wave shadow 103°-142°, S-wave shadow beyond 103°, proves liquid outer core
Body waves vs surface waves - difference in propagation and damage potential
Wave refraction and reflection at discontinuities - explains shadow zones
Richter Scale - measures amplitude of seismic waves, logarithmic, local magnitude (ML)
Moment Magnitude Scale (Mw) - better for large earthquakes, measures seismic moment
Seismograph - instrument to detect and record seismic waves, types (mechanical, electronic)
Seismic ray paths - straight in homogeneous medium, curved in heterogeneous medium
Critical angle and refraction of seismic waves - mathematical concept
Velocity discontinuities - cause of wave refraction, marks layer boundaries
Using seismic data to map interior - velocity changes at different depths
Velocity reversal at core-mantle boundary and liquid outer core
Modern seismic networks - global distribution for accurate earthquake location
important_diagrams: P-waves and S-waves travel paths through Earth - labeled diagram
Seismic shadow zones - showing P-wave and S-wave shadows on Earth cross-section
Seismograph recordings showing P, S, and L waves arrival times
Epicentre and hypocenter (focus) location diagram
Richter Scale magnitude chart with examples
Seismic wave velocity with depth graph
P-wave and S-wave velocity changes at major discontinuities
topics.map_work: Not applicable for seismic waves

key_concepts: Plate tectonics definition - theory explaining Earth's lithospheric structure and dynamics
Lithosphere - rigid outer layer comprising crust and upper mantle, 50-100 km thick
Asthenosphere - zone of partial melting beneath lithosphere, allows plate movement
Major tectonic plates - Pacific, North American, Eurasian, African, Antarctic, Indo-Australian, South American
Minor plates - Caribbean, Cocos, Nazca, Philippine, Scotia plates
Hotspot volcanism - mantle plumes, Hawaii-Emperor seamount chain, Yellowstone
Mantle plumes - hot rising columns of mantle material, stationary relative to mantle
Triple junctions - point where three plates meet, types: RRR, TRR, TTT
Plate boundaries - convergent, divergent, transform, classification and significance
Spreading rates - fast (>10 cm/yr), intermediate (5-10 cm/yr), slow (<5 cm/yr) ridges
Transform faults - tear faults connecting offset ridge segments, San Andreas example
Subduction zones - dipping plane 30-60°, B-B type (benioff zone)
Obduction - reverse of subduction, rare, ophiolite sequences
Slab pull vs ridge push - driving forces of plate movement
Plate motion velocities - measured by GPS, 2-20 cm/year relative motion
important_diagrams: Map of world's major and minor tectonic plates
Three types of plate boundaries - convergent, divergent, transform with cross-sections
Divergent boundary - mid-ocean ridge, rift valley formation
Convergent boundaries - three types (oceanic-oceanic, oceanic-continental, continental-continental)
Transform boundary - strike-slip fault diagram
Hotspot track diagram - Emperor seamounts and Hawaiian Islands
Mantle plume beneath hotspot - vertical cross-section
Triple junction types - RRR, TRR, TTT diagrams
Plate motion vectors - showing direction and speed of movement
topics.map_work: Mark all major and minor tectonic plates, divergent boundaries (mid-ocean ridges), convergent boundaries (subduction zones, collision zones), transform boundaries (San Andreas, Dead Sea), hotspot locations (Hawaii, Yellowstone, Iceland, Reunion)

key_concepts: Continental drift theory - Wegener (1915), continents move horizontally over time
Evidence 1: Fit of continents - jigsaw puzzle fit of South America and Africa coastlines
Evidence 2: Rock and mineral similarities - identical rock types in matching continents
Evidence 3: Fossil evidence - Mesosaurus (freshwater), Lystrosaurus, Glossopteris distribution
Evidence 4: Paleomagnetic evidence - polar wandering curves, magnetic reversals, seafloor stripes
Evidence 5: Paleoclimatic evidence - glacial deposits in equatorial zones, coal deposits in Antarctica
Evidence 6: Structural similarities - mountain ranges alignment across continents (Appalachians-Caledonian)
Pangea - supercontinent 250 million years ago, fragmentation into Laurasia and Gondwana
Seafloor spreading - Hess (1962), plates separate at mid-ocean ridges, new crust forms
Magnetic anomalies - striped pattern of normal and reversed magnetism on seafloor
Pillow lava - extrusive igneous rock formed at mid-ocean ridges underwater
Age dating of oceanic crust - radiometric dating, oldest >180 million years, youngest at ridges
Paleomagnetic reversals - magnetic poles reverse 1-10 times per million years
Chronostratigraphic timescale - correlation of magnetic reversals with geological time periods
Sediment thickness variation - thicker away from ridges, supports spreading theory
Modern measurement - GPS/VLBI - directly measures continental drift (~2-10 cm/year)
important_diagrams: Pangea and fragmentation into continents through time - multiple snapshots (250, 200, 100 Ma)
Continental drift evidence summary - all six pieces of evidence illustrated
Matching coastlines and geological features across Atlantic
Fossil distribution maps - Mesosaurus, Lystrosaurus, Glossopteris
Paleomagnetic polar wandering curves for different continents
Seafloor spreading at mid-ocean ridge - vertical cross-section with new crust formation
Magnetic anomaly stripes on seafloor - showing normal and reversed patterns
Age of oceanic crust map - color-coded age progression from ridge
Comparison of continental and oceanic crust - age vs distance from ridge plot
topics.map_work: Mark Pangea position, Laurasia and Gondwana, modern continents, Atlantic Ocean mid-ocean ridge, distribution of fossils (Mesosaurus, Lystrosaurus, Glossopteris), glacial deposits in Southern Hemisphere

key_concepts: Earthquake definition - sudden shaking of Earth's crust due to energy release
Elastic rebound theory - stress accumulation and sudden release in crustal rocks
Types of earthquakes: Tectonic (plate boundaries, 90%), Volcanic (near magma), Explosion (nuclear)
Focus/Hypocenter - exact location of energy release, depth measured from surface
Epicentre - point on surface directly above focus, where damage is maximum
Depth classification: Shallow (<70 km), Intermediate (70-300 km), Deep (>300 km)
Magnitude - measure of energy released, logarithmic scale (Richter, moment magnitude, others)
Richter Scale (Local Magnitude/ML) - amplitude of seismic waves, only for shallow earthquakes <600 km
Moment Magnitude Scale (Mw) - seismic moment = rigidity × area × slip, better for large earthquakes
Energy-magnitude relationship - increase of 1.0 on Richter ≈ 30 times more energy
Intensity - qualitative measure of earthquake effects (MMI Scale, I-XII), varies with location
Earthquake zones: Convergent plate boundaries (70%), Transform boundaries (20%), Intraplate (10%)
Foreshocks - small earthquakes preceding main shock, not always reliable
Aftershocks - earthquakes after main shock, decrease in frequency and magnitude over time
Earthquake swarms - multiple earthquakes in short period, often near volcanoes
Earthquake prediction - current limitations, warnings vs forecasting, historical patterns
important_diagrams: Focus and epicentre diagram with depth classification
Seismic wave arrival times and calculation of epicentre distance
Richter Scale magnitude chart with examples (1-9)
Moment Magnitude vs Richter Scale comparison
Modified Mercalli Intensity Scale (I-XII) with damage descriptions
Elastic rebound theory - before, during, after energy release
Global distribution of earthquakes - showing plate boundary concentration
Depth of earthquakes at different plate boundaries - Benioff zone diagram
Energy released vs magnitude logarithmic relationship graph
topics.map_work: Mark major earthquake zones: Ring of Fire, Mid-ocean ridges, Collision zones (Himalaya), Transform boundaries (San Andreas, Dead Sea), Intraplate zones (stable continental interiors)

key_concepts: Volcano definition - vent or fissure in Earth's crust where magma erupts
Magma composition - basaltic (low silica, low viscosity), andesitic (intermediate), rhyolitic (high silica, high viscosity)
Shield volcano - gentle slopes (2-10°), low viscosity basaltic lava, Hawaiian type, example: Hawaii, Kilauea
Cinder cone - steep slopes (30-40°), ejecta-dominated, small height (100-400 m), example: Paricutin
Composite/Stratovolcano - alternating layers of lava and ejecta, steep slopes, explosive, example: Vesuvius, Mount Fuji
Caldera - large depression from collapse or explosion, >2 km diameter, example: Crater Lake, Yellowstone
Lava types: Aa (rough, blocky), Pahoehoe (smooth, ropy), Pillow (underwater)
Pyroclastic materials: Ash (<2 mm), Lapilli (2-64 mm), Bombs (>64 mm)
Volcanic gases: CO2, H2O, SO2, HCl, H2S, role in climate change
Hotspot volcanism - magma plumes, intraplate volcanism, Yellowstone, Hawaii-Emperor chain
Ring of Fire - circum-Pacific belt, 75% of active volcanoes, due to subduction zones
Rift zone volcanism - associated with divergent plate boundaries, Iceland, East African Rift
Collision zone volcanism - continental-continental collision, Cascade Range, Andes
Monogenetic vs polygenetic volcanoes - single eruption vs multiple eruptions
Volcanic hazards - lava flows, pyroclastic flows, lahars, volcanic bombs, ash fall, toxic gases
important_diagrams: Shield volcano cross-section and profile
Cinder cone cross-section and profile
Composite/Stratovolcano cross-section showing layers
Caldera formation sequence - subsidence diagram
Lava types comparison - Aa, Pahoehoe, Pillow
Pyroclastic flow diagram with temperature and velocity
Magma composition triangle - basaltic, andesitic, rhyolitic
Viscosity vs silica content graph
Global distribution of volcanoes - Ring of Fire and hotspots marked
Volcanic eruption types - Hawaiian, Strombolian, Vulcanian, Plinian
Lahar formation diagram - along river valleys
topics.map_work: Mark Ring of Fire, major active volcanoes (Mount Fuji, Vesuvius, Krakatoa, Mount Pinatubo, Eyjafjallajökull), hotspot volcanoes (Hawaii, Yellowstone), rift zone volcanoes (Iceland), collision zone volcanoes (Andes, Cascade Range), calderas (Crater Lake, Yellowstone)

revision_tasks.day_1: Draw and label all river landforms - V-valley, meander, oxbow lake, delta
revision_tasks.day_2: Glacial landforms - U-valley, cirque, horn, moraine types
revision_tasks.day_3: Wind and coastal landforms - dunes, spits, cliffs, caves

key_concepts: Mineral definition - naturally occurring, inorganic, crystalline solid with definite composition
Rock definition - natural aggregate of minerals, can be monomineralic or polymineralic
Igneous rocks - form from magma cooling, intrusive (plutonic) vs extrusive (volcanic)
Granite - coarse-grained igneous, formed from slow cooling underground, example of intrusive
Basalt - fine-grained igneous, formed from fast cooling at surface, example of extrusive
Rhyolite - acid igneous, high silica content, viscous lava, fast cooling
Sedimentary rocks - form from compaction and cementation of sediments, clastic and chemical
Sandstone - clastic sedimentary, medium-grained, quartz-rich, permeable
Limestone - chemical sedimentary, calcium carbonate, organic origin, soluble in acids
Shale - clastic sedimentary, finest grains, formed from mud, impermeable
Metamorphic rocks - form under heat and pressure, non-foliated and foliated types
Slate - foliated metamorphic, from shale, splits into thin sheets, used for roofing
Marble - non-foliated metamorphic, from limestone, used for sculpture and building
Schist - foliated metamorphic, medium-grade, parallel arrangement of minerals
Gneiss - foliated metamorphic, high-grade, banding of light and dark minerals
important_diagrams: Rock Cycle diagram - showing pathways between igneous, sedimentary, metamorphic rocks
Igneous rock identification chart - grain size vs silica content
Bowen's reaction series - crystallization sequence of minerals
Sedimentary rock classification - clastic (by grain size) and chemical types
Metamorphic rock facies diagram - temperature vs pressure conditions
Foliated vs non-foliated metamorphic rocks - comparison
Mineral crystal structures - basic crystal systems
Rock texture classification - phaneritic, aphanitic, porphyritic, glassy
topics.map_work: Not directly applicable, but identify rock types in regional geology

key_concepts: Weathering definition - in-situ breakdown of rocks without transportation
Mechanical weathering - physical disintegration, no chemical change, rock reduces in size
Frost wedging/Freeze-thaw - water in cracks freezes, expands, breaks rock apart
Exfoliation - outer layers peel off due to pressure release, granite domes, onion-skin pattern
Salt weathering - crystallization of salts in pores, expansion breaks rock, common in arid regions
Abrasion - rocks rubbed together by wind, water, ice, creates rounded boulders
Thermal expansion - unequal heating causes stress, more in desert with day-night temperature difference
Chemical weathering - chemical reactions alter rock composition, rock dissolves or changes
Oxidation - combination with oxygen, iron minerals oxidize to rust (limonite)
Hydrolysis - reaction with water, feldspar converts to clay minerals
Carbonation - reaction with carbonic acid in water, limestone dissolves, karst formation
Biological weathering - organisms break down rocks, roots penetrate, lichen acids
Mass wasting definition - movement of rock/soil downslope under gravity
Types: Creep (slow, continuous), Landslide (fast, sudden), Slump (curved surface), Mudflow (wet)
Factors affecting mass wasting: Slope angle, rock type, weathering degree, water content, vegetation
important_diagrams: Types of mechanical weathering - frost wedging, exfoliation, salt weathering, abrasion
Chemical weathering processes - oxidation, hydrolysis, carbonation examples
Biological weathering agents - roots, lichen, burrowing animals
Mass wasting types - creep, slide, slump, mudflow, rockfall cross-sections
Landslide anatomy - scarp, toe, material on slope
Soil profile showing weathered material
Stability angle vs slope angle for mass wasting occurrence
Factors affecting slope stability - rainfall, earthquakes, human modification
topics.map_work: Identify areas prone to landslides, weathering patterns in different climates, erosion hotspots

key_concepts: River systems - dendritic, trellis, rectangular, radial drainage patterns
Young/Youth stage - V-shaped valley, vertical erosion, high velocity, rapids, waterfalls
V-shaped valley - narrow steep-sided valley formed in resistant rocks
Waterfall - abrupt descent, plunge pool at base, headward retreat due to erosion
Gorge - deep narrow valley with vertical or near-vertical walls, resistant rock
Mature/Middle stage - meanders develop, lateral erosion exceeds vertical, meandering channels
Meander - sinuous curve in river course, amplitude increases downstream, radius of curvature
Oxbow lake - crescent-shaped lake formed from meander cutoff, oxbow development stages
Floodplain - broad flat area adjacent to channel, inundated during floods, fertile soil
Levees - natural embankments on floodplain, artificial levees for flood control
Terraces - remnants of old floodplains, evidence of river incision, paired and unpaired
Old/Senile stage - low gradient, wide floodplain, braided channels, deltas
Delta - triangular or fan-shaped depositional landform at river mouth, prograding shoreline
Types of deltas: Arcuate (Nile), Bird's foot (Mississippi), Fan/Cuspate (Ganges)
Alluvial fan - fan-shaped depositional landform at mountain front, piedmont zone
important_diagrams: Three stages of river erosion - youth, mature, old age with valley cross-sections
V-shaped valley cross-section and longitudinal profile
Waterfall and gorge formation sequence
Meander formation and migration - point bar and river cliff
Oxbow lake formation stages - meander development to cutoff
Floodplain and terrace cross-section - showing old and new channels
Delta types - Arcuate, Bird's foot, Cuspate, Fan-shaped
Alluvial fan cross-section at mountain front
Braided channel network in old age rivers
River competence and capacity curves with grain size
Drainage patterns - dendritic, trellis, rectangular, radial, annular
topics.map_work: Mark major river systems (Nile, Mississippi, Amazon, Ganges-Brahmaputra, Yangtze), deltas (Nile, Mississippi, Ganges, Mekong), river gorges (Grand Canyon, Yangtze Gorges), famous waterfalls (Niagara, Victoria, Angel), meandering sections, floodplains, terraces

key_concepts: Glacier definition - slowly moving mass of ice on land, forms from accumulated snow
Snowline - altitude above which snow persists year-round, varies with latitude and local conditions
Equilibrium line - boundary between accumulation and ablation zones
Valley glacier/Alpine glacier - confined to mountains, flows downslope
Continental ice sheet - large area glacier, >50,000 km², covers plateau/continent
Glacial eroding power - abrasion (sandpaper effect), plucking (quarrying of rock)
U-shaped valley - characteristic glacial landform, vertical side walls, flat floor, wide opening
Cirque - amphitheater-shaped depression at glacier head, steep headwall, tarn lake
Arête - sharp ridge between adjacent cirques, knife-edge appearance
Horn - pyramidal peak where three or more cirques meet, Matterhorn example
Moraine - unsorted glacial deposit, types: lateral (valley sides), medial (where glaciers merge), terminal (glacier terminus)
Drumlin - elongated oval hill, streamlined in direction of glacier flow, length:width = 2-3:1
Esker - sinuous ridge of gravel/sand, former subglacial stream channel
Kame - irregular hill, deposited in ice-contact setting, material from meltwater
Outwash plain/Sandur - extensive flat plain of meltwater deposits, stratified material
important_diagrams: U-shaped vs V-shaped valley comparison - cross-section
Glacier cross-section showing accumulation and ablation zones
Cirque with backwall, floor, and tarn lake - 3D diagram
Arête formation between adjacent cirques
Horn formation - three cirques meeting
Moraine types - lateral, medial, terminal, ground moraine distribution
Drumlin orientation and shape - map view and profile
Esker and kame - subglacial vs ice-contact features
Outwash plain extent from glacier terminus
Glacial erosion features - striae, grooves, polish on bedrock
Hanging valley where tributary glacier meets main glacier valley
topics.map_work: Mark major glaciated regions (Alps, Rocky Mountains, Himalayas, Andes, Greenland, Antarctica), glacial lakes (Matterhorn area), drumlin fields (Ireland, Scotland), esker networks, outwash plains, moraines

key_concepts: Aeolian processes - wind erosion, transport, deposition in desert and coastal areas
Deflation - removal of fine particles by wind, forms deflation hollows
Abrasion - wind-blown sand acts as abrasive, creates ventifacts and desert varnish
Dune definition - accumulation of sand transported and deposited by wind
Barchan dune - crescent-shaped, horns pointing downwind, forms on hard surface
Seif dune - longitudinal dune, parallel to wind direction, extensive length
Star dune - multiple arms radiating from central peak, winds from multiple directions
Parabolic dune - U-shaped, horns point upwind, forms near coast or deflation hollow
Transverse dune - perpendicular to wind direction, forms where sand is abundant
Yardang - streamlined wind-eroded ridge, narrow parallel ridges separated by corridors
Pediment - gently sloping erosional surface at mountain base, covers bedrock
Inselberg - isolated rocky hill rising abruptly from pediment, residual hill
Reg/Hamada - gravel plains and rocky plateaus in desert, deflated surfaces
Ventifacts - pebbles shaped by wind abrasion, faceted appearance
Desert varnish - thin dark coating on rock surface, manganese and iron oxides
important_diagrams: Barchan dune shape and migration - plan view and cross-section
Seif dune - longitudinal pattern and cross-section
Star dune - radial arms pattern
Parabolic dune shape and relationship to deflation hollow
Transverse dune field arrangement
Yardang formation and alignment with wind direction
Pediment and inselberg relationship at mountain base
Ventifact faceting from wind abrasion
Desert landform association diagram - showing all features together
Sand transport - creep, saltation, suspension by wind
topics.map_work: Mark major deserts (Sahara, Arabian, Kalahari, Gobi, Simpson), dune field distributions, pediments, inselbergs (Ayers Rock), ergs, regs, yardang fields

key_concepts: Wave formation - wind-driven, friction creates waves, crest and trough terminology
Wave characteristics - wavelength, wave height, period, frequency
Wave refraction - waves bend in shallow water, focus on headlands, dissipate in bays
Wave breaking - breaker forms when depth = 1.3 × wave height
Wave-cut platform - erosional surface created by wave action at base of cliff
Sea cliff - steep coastal slope formed by wave erosion, undercuts and collapse
Sea cave - excavated by wave erosion in weak rock layers, may reach through rock
Sea arch - cave enlarged until roof collapses, creates tunnel through headland
Sea stack - isolated rock pillar, remnant of arch after roof collapse
Coastal deposition - sand and shingle transported and deposited in low-energy zones
Beach - accumulation of sediment (sand/shingle), active and inactive zones
Spit - curved ridge of sediment extending into sea/bay, formed by longshore drift
Bar - ridge of sediment connecting two headlands or island to mainland
Lagoon - body of water separated from sea by bar or barrier island
Delta - depositional landform at river mouth, prograding into sea/lake
important_diagrams: Wave anatomy - crest, trough, wavelength, wave height
Wave refraction around headland and bay
Wave-cut platform formation and cliff retreat
Sea cave, arch, and stack sequence - stages of development
Destructive vs constructive waves - swash and backwash
Beach profile - foreshore, backshore, berm, berms
Longshore drift - sediment transport along coast
Spit formation and growth sequence
Bar and lagoon formation
Coastal management - groyne, seawall, breakwater effectiveness
Tidal influence on coastal landforms
topics.map_work: Mark famous coastal features (White Cliffs of Dover, Twelve Apostles, Bay of Bengal delta), coral reefs, coastal spits and bars, lagoons, bays with wave-cut platforms, delta coasts

key_concepts: Karst definition - landscape developed on limestone/dolomite through solution weathering
Karst development requirements - soluble rock, adequate rainfall, permeable cover
Carbonation process - CO2 + H2O forms weak carbonic acid, dissolves limestone
Sinkhole/Doline - funnel-shaped depression from roof collapse of underground cave
Dolines types - solution sinkholes, subsidence sinkholes, collapse sinkholes
Disappearing stream - stream sinks into ground through sinkhole, reappears elsewhere
Stalactite - ice-cream cone shaped, hangs from ceiling, forms from dripping water
Stalagmite - builds upward from floor, resembles inverted cone, thicker than stalactite
Columns - stalactite and stalagmite join together
Cave formation - elongated cavern enlarged by water erosion, often follows joint/fault
Flowstone - sheet-like deposits on cave floor or walls
Rimstone pools - deposits forming barriers along cave streams
Polje - large closed depression in limestone, flat floor, seasonal lake
Limestone pavement - fissured surface from dissolution along joints, clints and grykes
Travertine terraces - hot spring deposits forming stepped pools and barriers
important_diagrams: Karst landscape cross-section - sinkholes, caves, underground streams
Stalactite vs stalagmite formation from cave ceiling drips
Column formation - stalactite and stalagmite meeting
Sinkhole formation stages - solution, subsidence, collapse
Cave formation in limestone - joints, faults, solution pathways
Polje features - closed depression, flat floor, margins
Limestone pavement - clints and grykes pattern
Travertine terrace formation at hot springs
Karst valley development - losing stream to underground aquifer
Solution features at different depths - surface vs subsurface
topics.map_work: Mark major karst regions (Mediterranean, Southeast China, USA South, Vietnam), famous caves (Carlsbad, Ajanta), sinkholes, travertine deposits (Pamukkale), polje areas

revision_tasks.day_1: Atmospheric structure and composition - memorize altitude boundaries
revision_tasks.day_2: Pressure belts and wind systems - ITCZ, horse latitudes, wind patterns
revision_tasks.day_4: Koppen climate classification - all types with examples
revision_tasks.day_5: Full mock test - comprehensive atmosphere and climate

key_concepts: Atmosphere - envelope of air surrounding Earth, held by gravity, mass concentrated in lower layers
Composition - Nitrogen 78.08%, Oxygen 20.95%, Argon 0.93%, CO2 0.04%, Neon, Helium, other gases trace
Variable gases - Water vapor (0-4%), Dust/aerosols, Pollutants - affect weather and climate
Troposphere - lowest layer, 0-8-18 km altitude, temperature decreases with altitude, contains 75% mass
Lapse rate - rate of temperature decrease, average 6.5°C per km (environmental lapse rate)
Stratosphere - 8-50 km altitude, temperature increases with altitude due to ozone absorption
Ozone layer - concentrated at 15-35 km, absorbs UV radiation, protects life
Mesosphere - 50-80 km altitude, temperature decreases, coldest layer
Thermosphere - 80-500 km altitude, temperature increases, includes ionosphere
Exosphere - beyond 500 km altitude, transition to space, extremely thin
Atmospheric density - decreases exponentially with altitude, 50% mass below 5.5 km
Pressure - weight of air column above, decreases with altitude, measured in mb or Pa
Air masses - large bodies of air with uniform temperature and humidity
Jet streams - narrow high-speed wind belts at tropopause, subtropical and polar varieties
Atmospheric circulation cells - Hadley, Ferrel, Polar cells drive global wind patterns
important_diagrams: Atmospheric layers - altitude zones with temperature profiles
Composition of atmosphere - pie chart with percentages
Temperature profile with altitude showing inversion layers
Pressure decrease with altitude - exponential curve
Density variation with altitude
Ozone concentration with altitude
Atmospheric circulation cells - three-cell model
Jet stream locations - subtropical and polar jets
Air mass source regions - continental and maritime types
topics.map_work: Not directly applicable, but understand jet stream positions and air mass source regions

key_concepts: Insolation - incoming solar radiation reaching Earth's surface
Solar constant - 1,370 W/m² at top of atmosphere, varies ±3% with solar activity
Factors affecting insolation: latitude (angle of incidence), season, time of day, atmosphere clarity
Angle of incidence - vertical rays equator, oblique rays poles, determines intensity
Atmospheric depletion - ozone absorption UV, scattering by gases/particles, clouds reflect/absorb
Albedo - fraction of incident radiation reflected, varies by surface (snow 80%, ocean 10%)
Heat absorption - dark surfaces absorb more (low albedo), light surfaces reflect more
Atmospheric window - shortwave radiation passes through, longwave absorbed by greenhouse gases
Earth radiation - longwave radiation emitted from Earth's surface and atmosphere
Greenhouse effect - greenhouse gases (CO2, CH4, H2O, N2O) trap longwave radiation, warm atmosphere
Heat budget - balance between incoming and outgoing radiation, near equilibrium globally
Radiation balance - tropics receive more than needed, poles receive less, poleward heat transport
Terrestrial radiation curve - peak in infrared region, shifts with temperature
Stefan-Boltzmann Law - relationship between temperature and radiation emitted
Radiative equilibrium - temperature where incoming = outgoing radiation
important_diagrams: Insolation distribution with latitude - showing maximum at equator
Solar radiation budget diagram - atmospheric absorption, reflection, transmission percentages
Earth's heat budget - incoming shortwave, outgoing longwave, albedo losses
Atmospheric window and greenhouse effect - radiation trapping diagram
Angle of solar incidence at different latitudes
Daily and seasonal insolation variation with latitude
Albedo values for different surfaces - comparison chart
Temperature vs radiation emitted - Stefan-Boltzmann curve
Atmospheric layers and radiation interaction
topics.map_work: Understand insolation variation with latitude, seasonal changes, influence on climate zones

key_concepts: Temperature - measure of average kinetic energy of molecules, measured in °C or K
Diurnal temperature range - difference between daily max and min, larger in clear, dry conditions
Annual temperature range - difference between warmest and coldest months, larger in continental climates
Lapse rate - rate of temperature change with altitude, varies with atmospheric conditions
Dry adiabatic lapse rate (DALR) - 9.8°C/km or 10°C/km, unsaturated air
Saturated adiabatic lapse rate (SALR) - 4-6°C/km, saturated air, latent heat release
Environmental lapse rate (ELR) - actual observed rate, averages 6.5°C/km
Temperature inversion - temperature increases with altitude, stable atmosphere, common in winter
Thermal inversion effects - traps pollutants, fog formation, stable conditions
Urban heat island - cities warmer than surrounding countryside, 2-5°C difference
Causes of UHI - reduced vegetation, dark surfaces, waste heat, reduced wind
Isothermal lines - connect places of equal temperature on maps
Kontinentality - temperature extremes in continental interiors, moderated on coasts
Thermal equator - line of highest mean temperature, shifts with seasons, not at geographic equator
Temperature anomalies - warmer or cooler than expected for latitude
important_diagrams: Lapse rate types - DALR, SALR, ELR comparison
Temperature inversion - temperature increasing with altitude
Diurnal temperature curve - max, min, mean variation
Annual temperature curve - seasonal variation
Urban heat island - temperature difference from city center to rural areas
Isothermal map of world - January and July patterns
Temperature gradient with latitude - annual mean
Thermal equator position - January vs July
Continentality index - temperature range vs latitude plot
topics.map_work: Mark isothermal patterns (January and July), thermal equator shifts, cold currents (lower temperature), warm currents (higher temperature), major temperature anomalies

key_concepts: Atmospheric pressure - weight of air column above, decreases with altitude exponentially
Pressure units - millibars (mb), hectopascals (hPa), 1 mb = 1 hPa, 1 atm = 1013.25 mb
Isobars - lines of equal pressure on maps, spacing indicates pressure gradient
Pressure gradient - pressure change per unit distance, drives wind movement
High pressure system - anticyclone, air sinks, clear skies, divergent winds
Low pressure system - cyclone, air rises, cloud formation, convergent winds
ITCZ (Intertropical Convergence Zone) - near equator, low pressure, converging trade winds
Horse latitudes - 30°N/S, high pressure belts, subsiding air, calm winds, deserts form here
Subpolar low-pressure belts - 60°N/S, low pressure, converging westerlies and polar easterlies
Polar high-pressure region - poles, descending air, clear skies, divergent polar easterlies
Trade winds - consistent winds from 30° to equator, NE in Northern Hemisphere, SE in Southern
Westerlies - winds from 30-60°, SW in Northern Hemisphere, NW in Southern Hemisphere
Polar easterlies - winds from poles to 60°, E direction at both poles
Jet streams - narrow high-speed wind bands at tropopause, subtropical (27-30°) and polar (60°) jets
Monsoon winds - seasonal wind reversal, driven by differential heating of land and ocean
important_diagrams: Pressure zones at different latitudes - ITCZ, horse latitudes, subpolar, polar
Wind belts - trade winds, westerlies, polar easterlies, isobars
High and low pressure systems - anticyclone and cyclone structures
Coriolis effect - deflection of winds, circular motion of pressure systems
Buys Ballot's Law - relationship between wind and pressure
Jet stream positions - subtropical and polar jets
Monsoon circulation - seasonal reversal pattern
Pressure gradient and wind relationship - isobar spacing
Upper-level and surface pressure patterns - differences
topics.map_work: Mark ITCZ position (January and July), pressure belts (high and low), trade wind and westerly wind zones, jet stream positions, monsoon directions, pressure systems

key_concepts: Water vapor - gaseous form of water, source of all precipitation, invisible
Humidity - amount of water vapor in air, measured as absolute or relative humidity
Absolute humidity - mass of water vapor per unit volume, g/m³, increases with temperature
Relative humidity - ratio of actual vapor pressure to saturation vapor pressure, expressed in %
Dew point - temperature at which air becomes saturated (RH = 100%), depends on moisture content
Saturation vapor pressure - maximum vapor pressure at given temperature, increases exponentially
Condensation - phase change from vapor to liquid when air cools below dew point
Hygroscopic nuclei - particles (dust, salt, smoke) on which condensation begins
Cloud formation - adiabatic cooling, orographic lifting, frontal lifting, convergence
Lapse rates and cloud formation - ELR vs SALR determines stability
Precipitation - water in liquid or solid form falling from clouds
Convectional precipitation - thunderstorms from surface heating, afternoon peak
Orographic precipitation - air forced upward by mountains, windward side wet, leeward dry
Cyclonic precipitation - frontal systems, warm and cold fronts
Precipitation types - rain, snow, sleet, hail, freezing rain
Acid rain - precipitation with pH <5.6, from SO2 and NOx pollution
important_diagrams: Humidity definitions - absolute, relative, saturation vapor pressure
Dew point and relative humidity relationship with temperature
Adiabatic cooling and cloud formation - DALR and SALR
Stability and instability - ELR vs lapse rates
Cloud types - cumulus, stratus, cirrus, cumulonimbus formations
Condensation process - nucleation and droplet growth
Convectional precipitation diagram - heating and updrafts
Orographic precipitation - windward and leeward slopes
Cyclonic/frontal precipitation - warm and cold front structures
Precipitation types - rain, snow, sleet, hail formation conditions
Annual precipitation world map - distribution patterns
topics.map_work: Mark wettest regions, arid regions, orographic precipitation areas (windward mountains), monsoon precipitation patterns, cyclone tracks, snowfall regions

key_concepts: Cyclone definition - low-pressure system, converging winds, rotating motion
Anticyclone definition - high-pressure system, diverging winds, clear weather
Temperate/mid-latitude cyclone - frontal system, 1000-2000 km diameter, 3-5 days duration
Cold front - boundary where cold air mass advances, clouds, precipitation
Warm front - boundary where warm air mass advances, gradual lifting, drizzle
Occluded front - overlap of cold and warm fronts in mature cyclone
Tropical cyclone - storm over warm tropical ocean, warm core, rotating thunderstorms
Tropical cyclone requirements - SST >26.5°C, Coriolis force, low wind shear, high humidity
Typhoon/Cyclone/Hurricane - same phenomenon, different regional names
Saffir-Simpson scale - hurricane wind speed and damage classification
Eye of hurricane - calm center, extreme pressure drop, surrounded by eyewall
El Niño - warm oceanic phase of ENSO, reduced upwelling, global climate impacts
La Niña - cold oceanic phase of ENSO, enhanced upwelling, opposite impacts
ENSO (El Niño Southern Oscillation) - Pacific oscillation affecting global weather
Indian Ocean Dipole (IOD) - Indian Ocean warm-cold dipole affecting monsoon
important_diagrams: Temperate cyclone structure - isobars, fronts, cloud bands, wind direction
Cold front symbol and associated weather
Warm front symbol and associated weather
Occluded front formation and weather
Tropical cyclone structure - eye, eyewall, spiral rain bands
Tropical cyclone formation requirements - SST, latitude, wind shear
Saffir-Simpson scale - wind speed categories
Hurricane track prediction - typical paths in Atlantic and Pacific
ENSO phases - warm, neutral, cold states and global impacts
IOD index - positive and negative phases
Climate anomaly maps - temperature and precipitation anomalies
topics.map_work: Mark tropical cyclone formation zones, typical tracks (Atlantic, Pacific, Indian Ocean), temperate cyclone tracks, ENSO-affected regions, IOD influence areas, climate anomaly hotspots

key_concepts: Köppen classification - empirical climate classification using temperature and precipitation
Climate zones - 5 major types: A, B, C, D, E (tropical to polar)
A - Tropical: warm year-round, >18°C mean monthly, examples: Af (rain forest), Am (monsoon), Aw/As
Af - Tropical Rainforest: >60 mm rain every month, high humidity, Amazon, Congo
Am - Tropical Monsoon: short dry season, ITCZ influence, Southeast Asia
Aw/As - Tropical Savanna: distinct dry season, wet summer, Africa, Australia
B - Arid/Semi-arid: low precipitation, high evaporation, examples: BW (desert), BS (steppe)
BWh - Hot desert: rain <threshold, >18°C, Sahara, Arabian deserts
BWk - Cold desert: rain <threshold, <18°C, Gobi, Atacama
BS - Steppe: intermediate precipitation between desert and humid, transitional climate
C - Temperate: coldest month -3° to 18°C, examples: Csa (Mediterranean), Cfb (oceanic), Cw (dry winter)
Csa - Mediterranean: hot dry summer, mild wet winter, California, Mediterranean region
Cfb - Oceanic: temperate, no dry season, cool summers, marine influence, New Zealand, Scotland
D - Continental: coldest month <-3°C, examples: Dw (dry winter), Df (wet year-round)
Dfb - Humid continental: coldest <-3°C, warmest >10°C, 4+ months >10°C, Canada, Russia
E - Polar: all months <10°C, examples: ET (tundra), EF (ice cap)
ET - Tundra: 0-10°C warmest month, permafrost, Arctic, Siberia
EF - Ice cap: all months <0°C, permanent ice, Greenland, Antarctica
important_diagrams: Köppen climate zones - world map with all major zones colored
Climograph examples for each major climate type - temperature and precipitation curves
Climate classification criteria - temperature and precipitation thresholds
A zones - Af, Am, Aw distribution on map
B zones - BW and BS boundaries and distribution
C zones - Csa, Cfb, Cw distribution on map
D zones - Dfb and other continental distribution
E zones - ET and EF at poles
Vegetation-climate relationship - climate determines biome type
Altitudinal climate zones - mountain climate variations
topics.map_work: Mark all Köppen climate zones on world map, identify boundaries, major cities in each zone, climate transitions, examples of each type (Af-Amazon, BSk-steppes, Csa-Mediterranean, Dfb-continental, ET-tundra, EF-ice caps)

key_concepts: Climate change - long-term shift in mean temperature, precipitation, wind patterns
Natural causes - solar variations, orbital cycles, volcanic eruptions, ocean circulation
Anthropogenic causes - greenhouse gas emissions (CO2, CH4, N2O), deforestation, industrialization
CO2 sources - fossil fuel combustion (coal, oil, gas), cement production, deforestation
Greenhouse gas concentrations - CO2 rising from 280 ppm (pre-industrial) to 420+ ppm (2023)
Enhanced greenhouse effect - increased atmospheric CO2 traps more longwave radiation
Global warming - increase in mean global temperature, ~1.1°C above pre-industrial
Temperature projections - 1.5-4°C increase by 2100 depending on emissions scenario
Impacts on ice - melting glaciers, polar ice sheets, permafrost thaw, sea level rise
Sea level rise - thermal expansion of ocean water and ice sheet melting, 3-4 mm/year
Precipitation changes - altered patterns, intensity increases, monsoon modifications
Feedback mechanisms - positive (albedo, water vapor, carbon cycle) and negative feedbacks
Albedo feedback - less ice means lower albedo, more heat absorption, accelerates warming
Water vapor feedback - warmer air holds more vapor, increases greenhouse effect, positive feedback
Permafrost thaw - releases CO2 and CH4, accelerates warming, irreversible feedback
important_diagrams: Global temperature anomaly trend - showing recent rapid warming
CO2 concentration increase - ice core and direct measurements
Greenhouse gas sources - pie chart showing contributions
Temperature projections - IPCC scenarios for different emissions pathways
Sea level rise - historical and projected curves
Albedo feedback diagram - ice loss and surface heating
Carbon cycle perturbations - anthropogenic additions
Impacts of climate change - temperature, precipitation, ice sheets, biodiversity
Permafrost extent - current and projected loss
Feedback mechanisms - positive and negative loop diagrams
topics.map_work: Mark regions experiencing severe climate change effects (high mountain glaciers, Arctic, permafrost zones), sea level rise vulnerable coasts, climate-sensitive ecosystems, industrial emission hotspots

revision_tasks.day_1: Ocean relief features - continental shelf, slope, abyssal plain, trenches
revision_tasks.day_2: Ocean currents - warm and cold currents, major currents globally
revision_tasks.day_3: Tides, waves, coral reefs - formation and types

key_concepts: Ocean floor morphology - divided into continental margin, ocean basin, mid-ocean ridge
Continental shelf - gently sloping submerged margin of continent, 200 m depth, sediment accumulation
Shelf break - boundary between shelf and slope at ~200 m depth
Continental slope - steep descent from shelf to abyssal plain, 200-3000 m, sediment gravity flows
Continental rise - gentle slope at base of margin, coalesces slopes, submarine fan deposits
Abyssal plain - vast flat area at ~4000-5000 m depth, deepest ocean parts
Abyssal hills - low ridges on abyssal plain, sediment draped, few hundred meters high
Ocean trenches - deepest depressions, >6000 m, associated with subduction zones
Mariana Trench - deepest at ~11,000 m (Challenger Deep), western Pacific
Mid-ocean ridges - underwater mountain chains at divergent boundaries, 2500-3000 m depth
Ridge system length - 65,000 km globally, continuous underwater mountain range
Rift valley - depression along ridge axis, graben structure, seafloor spreading
Seamounts - isolated underwater mountains, volcanic origin, >1000 m elevation
Guyots - flat-topped seamounts, truncated by wave erosion, subsided below wave base
Hydrothermal vents - hot water and mineral discharge, 200-400°C, chemosynthetic ecosystem
important_diagrams: Ocean floor cross-section - continental margin to abyssal plain
Continental shelf, slope, and rise profile
Submarine canyon cross-section - cutting shelf and slope
Abyssal plain with abyssal hills - topographic features
Ocean trench - subduction zone association
Mid-ocean ridge anatomy - rift valley and volcanic features
Seamount and guyot compared
Black smoker hydrothermal vent structure
Ocean floor age map - seafloor spreading evidence
3D visualization of major submarine features
topics.map_work: Mark continental shelves (productive fishing areas), major ocean trenches (Mariana, Tonga, Kuril), mid-ocean ridge system, major seamounts and guyots, submarine canyons

key_concepts: Ocean temperature - varies with depth, latitude, season, affects density and circulation
Surface temperature - maximum in tropics (>25°C), decreases toward poles (<0°C)
Thermocline - zone of rapid temperature change, typically 200-1000 m depth
Deep water - near 0°C in abyssal depths, formed in polar regions
Temperature profile - surface warm, thermocline transition, abyssal cold
Salinity - total dissolved salt content, average 35 ppt (practical salinity units)
Factors affecting salinity - evaporation (increases), precipitation (decreases), river inflow (decreases)
Regional salinity variation - high in evaporative regions (Red Sea >41 ppt), low near river mouths
Halocline - zone of rapid salinity change, typically coincides with thermocline
Deep-water salinity - relatively uniform 34-35 ppt, formed by water mass mixing
Density stratification - density increases with depth, stability prevents mixing
Thermocline depth variation - shallower in tropics, deeper in high latitudes
Water mass types - classified by T-S characteristics, NADW, AABW, etc.
Isopycnal - surface of equal density, controls water mass boundaries
Deep water formation - sinking in polar regions, density-driven conveyor
important_diagrams: Ocean temperature profile - surface warm to abyssal cold
Thermocline location and depth variation with latitude
Salinity profile - surface salinity layer, halocline, deep uniform salinity
T-S diagram - classification of water masses
Density profile - stratification in water column
Global surface temperature distribution - warmest equator, coldest poles
Global surface salinity distribution - high evaporative regions, river mouths
Vertical sections - temperature, salinity, density together
Seasonal temperature variation - thermocline depth changes
topics.map_work: Mark surface temperature distribution (January and July), high salinity regions, river mouths with low salinity, areas of deep water formation (Weddell Sea, Labrador Sea)

key_concepts: Ocean current definition - continuous movement of water in definite direction
Warm currents - higher temperature, flow from equator toward poles
Cold currents - lower temperature, flow from poles toward equator
Surface currents - driven by wind, largest effect on upper 100-200 m
Deep currents - driven by density differences, thermohaline circulation
Ekman spiral - changing current direction with depth due to Coriolis effect
Gulf Stream - warm current, Florida to Newfoundland, part of Atlantic gyre
North Atlantic Drift - continuation of Gulf Stream, warms Western Europe
Labrador Current - cold Arctic water, moderates Eastern North American climate
Kuroshio Current - warm western boundary current, off Japan
Oyashio Current - cold current, off Russia, affects fishing
Humboldt/Peru Current - cold upwelling current, Peru-Chile coast, rich fishery, ENSO influence
Benguela Current - cold current, South Africa coast, upwelling support fishery
Agulhas Current - warm Indian Ocean current, circumpolar route
Antarctic Circumpolar Current - only current crossing ocean basins, no continental barrier
Thermohaline circulation - global conveyor, deep water formation and transport
important_diagrams: Surface ocean current map - global circulation patterns
Gyres - subtropical rotating systems in each ocean basin
Western boundary currents - Gulf Stream, Kuroshio, Brazil, Agulhas
Eastern boundary currents - cold upwelling currents
Ekman spiral - current direction rotation with depth
Thermohaline circulation - global conveyor belt path
Atlantic Meridional Overturning Circulation (AMOC) - north-south meridional flow
Upwelling areas - Peru, Benguela, California coasts
Temperature and salinity effects on density and circulation
Climate influence of ocean currents - Western Europe warming by North Atlantic Drift
topics.map_work: Mark all major ocean currents (warm: Gulf Stream, Kuroshio, Agulhas, Brazil; cold: Labrador, Oyashio, Humboldt, Benguela, California), gyres, upwelling zones, divergences, Antarctic Circumpolar Current

key_concepts: Waves - periodic oscillations of water surface, generated primarily by wind friction
Wave anatomy - crest, trough, wavelength, wave height, period, frequency
Wave velocity - speed of wave propagation, related to wavelength and period
Orbital motion - water particles move in circular paths, energy transfers, water doesn't move
Wave refraction - waves bend in shallow water due to bottom friction, focus on headlands
Wave breaking - occurs when depth = 1.3 × wave height, creates whitecap
Surf zone - area between breaker line and shore, turbulent water
Swash and backswash - water movement up and down beach, sediment transport
Swell - long-wavelength waves from distant storms, propagate far
Tsunamis - long-wavelength waves generated by earthquakes or landslides
Tsunami speed - 700+ km/hr in deep water, slows in shallow water
Tsunami characteristics - small in deep water, amplify in shallow water, destructive
Tides - regular rise and fall of sea level due to gravitational attraction of Moon and Sun
Tidal range - difference between high and low tide, spring tide (large), neap tide (small)
Semidiurnal tide - two high and low tides per lunar day (~24.8 hours)
important_diagrams: Wave anatomy - crest, trough, wavelength, wave height labeled
Wave refraction around headland and in bay
Orbital motion in deep and shallow water
Wave breaking sequence - shoaling, spilling, plunging
Tsunami propagation - shallow water amplification
Tidal forces - Moon and Sun gravitational pulls
Spring tide - Moon, Earth, Sun alignment
Neap tide - Moon, Earth, Sun at right angles
Semidiurnal vs diurnal tide patterns
Tidal current prediction - max current at mid-tide
Bore/tidal bore - upstream propagating tidal wave in rivers
topics.map_work: Mark major tsunami zones (Subduction zones), high tidal range areas (Bay of Fundy, Severn Estuary), tidal bore rivers (Amazon, Severn), wave-dominated coasts

key_concepts: Coral reef definition - rocky structures built by coral polyps, limestone skeleton
Scleractinian corals - hard corals with calcium carbonate skeletons, reef builders
Zooxanthellae - symbiotic algae in coral tissue, provide energy through photosynthesis
Coral growth requirements - warm water (20-29°C optimal), shallow sunlit water, clear water, moderate salinity
Fringing reef - reef attached directly to shore, narrow shallow lagoon
Barrier reef - reef parallel to shore with deeper lagoon, separated from shore
Atoll - ring-shaped reef with central lagoon, formed in deep ocean, Darwin's subsidence theory
Reef zonation - forereef slope (deep), reef crest (shallowest), reef flat, lagoon
Reef biodiversity - thousands of species, fish, mollusks, crustaceans, algae
Coral bleaching - symbiont expulsion from stress, linked to temperature anomalies
Bleaching causes - high temperature, low salinity, pollution, disease
Reef distribution - limited to tropical shallow oceans, 20°N-20°S generally
Major reefs - Great Barrier Reef, Mesoamerican Reef, Coral Triangle
Reef threats - climate change, ocean acidification, pollution, overfishing
Conservation - marine protected areas, restoration, management practices
important_diagrams: Fringing reef cross-section - shore to deep water
Barrier reef cross-section - reef crest, lagoon, forereef slope
Atoll formation sequence - Darwin's subsidence theory three stages
Reef zonation - different zones and coral/organism distribution
Coral polyp anatomy - tentacles, mouth, zooxanthellae
Bleaching process - symbiont loss diagram
Global coral reef distribution map
Reef biodiversity - major organism groups
Reef ecosystem relationships - food web, competition, symbiosis
topics.map_work: Mark major coral reefs (Great Barrier Reef, Mesoamerican, Coral Triangle, Red Sea, Indian Ocean), coral bleaching hotspots, reef distribution boundaries, conservation areas

revision_tasks.day_1: Soil formation factors - CLORPT, soil profile horizons
revision_tasks.day_2: Soil types - zonal, intrazonal, azonal with regional examples
revision_tasks.day_3: Biomes - tropical rainforest, savanna, desert, temperate, taiga, tundra
revision_tasks.day_5: Full mock test - soils and biogeography

key_concepts: Soil definition - mixture of weathered rock fragments, organic matter, water, air
CLORPT model - Cl (Climate), O (Organism), R (Relief), P (Parent material), T (Time)
Climate factor - temperature and precipitation control weathering and biological activity
Organism factor - vegetation, fauna, microorganisms contribute organic matter
Relief factor - slope angle affects drainage, water availability, erosion rate
Parent material - source rock type affects soil composition and properties
Time factor - soil develops over centuries to millennia, age affects maturity
Soil horizons - O, A, B, C, D layers with distinct characteristics
O horizon - organic layer, decomposing plant and animal material, surface layer
A horizon - topsoil, organic-rich, humus content, darker color, maximum biological activity
B horizon - subsoil, accumulation of clay and iron oxides, leached from A horizon
C horizon - weathered parent material, less altered than B, larger rock fragments
D horizon - unweathered bedrock, bottom of soil profile
Soil texture - proportion of sand, silt, clay, affects water retention and drainage
Soil structure - arrangement of soil particles into aggregates, crumbs, blocks, plates
Soil color - indicates composition and drainage, dark = organic rich, red = iron oxide
important_diagrams: CLORPT factors - soil formation controls
Soil profile - O, A, B, C, D horizons with transitions
Soil texture triangle - sand, silt, clay percentages
Soil structure types - granular, platy, blocky, prismatic shapes
Soil formation sequence - time series showing horizon development
Weathering rates - climate and parent material effects
Water infiltration and drainage in different soil types
Leaching process - water movement and ion transport downward
Soil color chart - Munsell system or visual reference
topics.map_work: Not directly applicable, but understand soil distribution with climate zones

key_concepts: Soil classification - zonal (climate-based), intrazonal (parent material-based), azonal (young)
Zonal soils - mature soils reflecting climatic conditions, regolith and soil together
Latosol/Laterite - tropical hot wet climate, highly weathered, iron and aluminum oxides, red color
Laterite formation - intense weathering, leaching of silica, iron/aluminum oxide concentration
Laterite characteristics - acidic, low fertility, hard when exposed to air, contains bauxite ore
Tropical red earth - similar to laterite but less extreme weathering, some nutrient retention
Chernozem/Black soil - temperate grassland climate, high humus content, dark color, CaCO3 at depth
Chernozem fertility - excellent fertility, moisture retention, agricultural potential
Podzol/Podsol - cool temperate forest climate, strong leaching, pale A horizon, iron-rich B horizon
Podzol characteristics - acidic, low fertility, bleached appearance, waterlogged areas
Brown earth/Brown forest soil - temperate deciduous forest, moderate weathering, brown color
Intrazonal soils - determined by local parent material or drainage, less climate-dependent
Alluvial soil - formed from river alluvium, young, developing soil, variable fertility
Hydromorphic soil - formed in waterlogged conditions, gley color, poor drainage
Azonal soils - very young, little weathering, parent material dominates, newborn landscapes
important_diagrams: Soil classification hierarchy - zonal, intrazonal, azonal categories
Latosol profile - leached surface, iron oxide accumulation
Chernozem profile - high organic matter, CaCO3 layer
Podzol profile - bleached A horizon, iron-rich B horizon
Soil distribution with climate zones - map and associations
Weathering intensity vs latitude - tropical most intense
Soil fertility by type - comparison chart
Hydromorphic soil - waterlogged conditions and gley formation
Alluvial soil - recent river deposits, stratification
topics.map_work: Mark major soil types (Latosol/Laterite - tropics, Chernozem - temperate grasslands, Podzol - cool temperate forests), alluvial soil distribution along major rivers, soil degradation hotspots

key_concepts: Tropical rainforest climate - equatorial location, Af in Köppen, >2000 mm annual rainfall
Rainforest seasonality - no dry season, constant warmth 25-27°C, high humidity
Tropical rainforest vegetation - broadleaf evergreen, high canopy, dense understory
Canopy structure - emergent layer, upper canopy, lower canopy, understory, forest floor
Biodiversity - highest on Earth, 50%+ of global species, high endemism
Nutrient cycling - rapid decomposition due to warmth, nutrients locked in vegetation
Soil characteristics - laterite and latosol, red color, low fertility, acidic, thin organic layer
Water cycle - high transpiration and precipitation, rapid cycling
Competition - intense for light, tall trees dominate, epiphytes common
Animal adaptations - arboreal, nocturnal, camouflage, bright colors warning toxins
Location - Amazon, Congo, Southeast Asia, Central America
Threats - deforestation for agriculture, logging, cattle ranching
Ecological importance - carbon storage, oxygen production, climate regulation
Indigenous peoples - depend on rainforest resources, traditional knowledge
Conservation - protected areas, sustainable forestry, REDD+ programs
important_diagrams: Tropical rainforest climate graph - evenly distributed high rainfall, warm year-round
Vertical structure - canopy layers and height distribution
Typical rainforest trees - examples with heights and canopy positions
Food web - energy flow from plants to carnivores
Nutrient cycling - rapid decomposition and plant uptake
Global distribution - equatorial belt showing rainforests
Deforestation trends - area loss over time
Carbon storage - biomass and soil carbon pools
Biodiversity hotspot - species richness per unit area
topics.map_work: Mark major rainforests (Amazon, Congo, Southeast Asia, Central America, Australia tropical, Madagascar), protected areas, deforestation hotspots, indigenous territories

key_concepts: Savanna climate - tropical savanna (Aw/As Köppen), distinct wet and dry seasons, 500-1500 mm rainfall
Savanna vegetation - grassland with scattered trees, acacia common, fire-adapted species
Savanna characteristics - low rainfall, high evaporation, seasonal flooding, nutrient-poor soil
Savanna fauna - large herbivores (elephants, zebras, antelopes), predators (lions, leopards), termites
Savanna location - Africa Sahel, India monsoon region, South America cerrado, Australia outback
Desert climate - hot arid (BWh) or cold arid (BWk) Köppen, <500 mm annual rainfall, high evaporation
Desert vegetation - xerophytic adapted, sparse, deep-rooted shrubs, succulents, annuals
Desert fauna - nocturnal, water conservation, burrowing, heat tolerance
Desert types - hot desert (Sahara, Arabian), cold desert (Gobi, Atacama), coastal deserts
Desert soil - thin, low organic matter, high salinity, low water availability
Mediterranean climate - Csa Köppen, hot dry summer, mild wet winter, 500-1000 mm rainfall
Mediterranean vegetation - sclerophyll (hard-leaved), drought-resistant, fire-adapted, aromatic
Mediterranean fauna - similar to temperate regions, endemic species common
Mediterranean location - Mediterranean region, California, central Chile, South Africa, Australia
Human modification - agriculture, urbanization, overgrazing, desertification in marginal areas
important_diagrams: Savanna climate graph - wet and dry seasons
Savanna vegetation profile - scattered trees and grass
Acacia tree adaptations - thorns, shallow roots, small leaves
Large herbivore migration - seasonal movement patterns
Desert climate graph - low, erratic rainfall
Desert vegetation - yucca, saguaro, acacia, Welwitschia
Desert water storage - root depth, succulent water content
Desert fauna - adaptations for heat and water conservation
Mediterranean climate graph - hot dry summer, mild winter
Mediterranean vegetation - cork oak, maquis shrubland, grasses
Global distribution - all four biomes mapped
topics.map_work: Mark major savannas (African Sahel, Indian monsoon, cerrado, outback), deserts (Sahara, Arabian, Gobi, Atacama, Namib, Kalahari), Mediterranean regions (Mediterranean basin, California, central Chile, South Africa, SW Australia)

key_concepts: Temperate grassland climate - Cfb or Cw Köppen, moderate rainfall 500-1000 mm, distinct seasons
Temperate grassland vegetation - herbaceous (grasses, forbs), no trees due to low moisture or fire
Temperate grassland fauna - large herbivores (bison, antelope), predators, prairie dogs, insects
Temperate grassland location - Great Plains USA, Pampas South America, Steppe central Asia, Veld South Africa
Temperate grassland soil - chernozem, high fertility, dark color, high humus
Temperate deciduous forest climate - Cfb Köppen, adequate rainfall 750-1500 mm, four distinct seasons
Deciduous forest vegetation - broadleaf deciduous trees (oak, maple, beech), shrub layer, herbaceous
Deciduous forest seasonality - deciduous in winter, green in summer, leaf litter decomposition
Deciduous forest fauna - squirrels, deer, bears, migratory birds, insects
Deciduous forest location - Eastern North America, Western Europe, Eastern Asia temperate regions
Temperate forest soil - brown earth, moderate humus, acidic, good fertility
Boreal/Taiga climate - Dfb or Dfc Köppen, cold winters <-10°C, short summer, 400-1000 mm rainfall
Boreal forest vegetation - coniferous evergreens (spruce, fir, pine), sparse deciduous birch, aspen
Boreal forest characteristics - low biodiversity, dense forest, permafrost in soils, acid litter
Boreal forest fauna - elk, moose, caribou, bears, migratory birds, insects in summer
important_diagrams: Temperate grassland climate graph - seasonal variation, moderate rainfall
Grassland vegetation - no trees due to drought/fire, herbaceous growth
Bison herds and predator-prey relationships
Temperate deciduous forest climate graph - four seasons, adequate rainfall
Forest canopy and understory structure
Seasonal changes - bare trees winter, full canopy summer
Deciduous forest fauna - hibernation, migration, adaptation strategies
Boreal taiga climate graph - cold winter, short summer
Coniferous forest - dense, tall trees, closed canopy
Permafrost and boreal soil characteristics
Global distribution - all three biomes mapped by latitude
topics.map_work: Mark temperate grasslands (Great Plains, Pampas, Steppe, Veld), deciduous forests (Eastern North America, Europe, Asia), boreal forests/taiga (Canada, Scandinavia, Russia), permafrost zones

key_concepts: Tundra climate - ET Köppen, all months <10°C, <300 mm annual precipitation, extreme cold
Tundra vegetation - dwarf plants, lichens, mosses, no trees, permafrost limitations
Permafrost - permanently frozen ground layer, active layer thaws seasonally, depth >300 m
Tundra characteristics - harsh conditions, short growing season 6-10 weeks, low biodiversity
Tundra fauna - reindeer/caribou, musk ox, bears, migratory birds, insects in summer, fish
Tundra location - Arctic polar regions (Arctic islands, northern Canada, Siberia, Greenland)
Alpine tundra - high mountain areas, similar climate but above tree line, isolated communities
Biodiversity hotspot - global regions with high endemism and species richness
Hotspot criteria - 1500+ endemic plant species, <2% intact habitat remaining
Major hotspots - Tropical Andes, Mesoamerica, Amazon, Atlantic Forest, Madagascar, Tropical Africa
Hotspot characteristics - biodiversity, high human pressure, urgent conservation need
Conservation hotspots - megadiverse countries, limited resources, prioritization essential
Ecosystem services - biodiversity provides food, medicine, climate regulation, flood control
Threats to biodiversity - habitat loss, climate change, pollution, overexploitation, invasive species
Conservation strategies - protected areas, sustainable use, restoration, education, enforcement
important_diagrams: Tundra climate graph - cold year-round, low precipitation
Permafrost diagram - permanently frozen layer, active layer thawing
Tundra vegetation - dwarf plants, lichens, mosses
Tundra food web - limited species, herbivores and carnivores
Global tundra distribution - polar regions and high mountains
Biodiversity hotspot map - global distribution of 35+ hotspots
Species richness gradient - highest tropics, lowest polar regions
Endemism patterns - isolated areas have unique species
Habitat loss trends - deforestation and land conversion
Conservation priority areas - hotspots with highest threat and value
topics.map_work: Mark tundra regions (Arctic, permafrost zones), major biodiversity hotspots (Tropical Andes, Mesoamerica, Amazon, Atlantic Forest, Madagascar, Western Ghats-Sri Lanka, Sundaland, Philippines, Congo Basin), marine hotspots, protected areas

mains_essay_themes.theme_1: Plate tectonics and its role in shaping Earth's landscape - mountains, volcanoes, earthquakes
mains_essay_themes.theme_2: Climate change - causes, impacts, feedback mechanisms, mitigation strategies
mains_essay_themes.theme_3: Ocean currents and their influence on climate and fishing - global thermohaline circulation
mains_essay_themes.theme_4: Natural hazards and disaster management - earthquakes, volcanoes, cyclones, tsunamis
mains_essay_themes.theme_5: Biomes and biogeography - adaptation to climate, distribution patterns, human impact
mains_essay_themes.theme_6: Soil formation and degradation - fertility, erosion, desertification, conservation
mains_essay_themes.theme_7: Water scarcity and hydrological cycle - groundwater, surface water, monsoon patterns
mains_essay_themes.theme_8: Geomorphology and river systems - landform development, erosion cycles, delta formation
mains_essay_themes.theme_9: Tropical forests and biodiversity conservation - ecosystem services, deforestation threats
mains_essay_themes.theme_10: Coastal geomorphology and marine ecosystems - coral reefs, mangroves, sea-level rise

standard_preparation_6_months.months_1_2: Modules 1-2: Interior of Earth, Plate Tectonics, Geomorphology
standard_preparation_6_months.month_6: Module 5: Soils and Biogeography + Revision and Mock Tests

weekly_breakdown.monday_wednesday_friday: Concept learning and detailed reading - 3 hours each day
weekly_breakdown.tuesday_thursday: Diagram practice and map work - 2.5 hours each day
weekly_breakdown.saturday: Module review and concept integration - 3 hours
weekly_breakdown.sunday: Weekly mock test or revision - 2-3 hours

study_tips.tip_1: Maps are crucial - practice marking all locations mentioned in every topic
study_tips.tip_2: Diagrams should be drawn from memory regularly - essential for mains and prelims
study_tips.tip_3: Link physical geography to current events - earthquakes, floods, droughts, climate news
study_tips.tip_4: Use NCERT extensively - Official standard for content accuracy and direct quotes
study_tips.tip_5: Practice previous year questions - identify patterns and frequently tested topics
study_tips.tip_6: Memorize key numbers - coordinates of major features, altitude ranges, temperature values
study_tips.tip_7: Understand cause-effect - Why mountains form, why deserts exist, why monsoonal rains
study_tips.tip_8: Relate topics - Plate tectonics → Mountains → Climate → Vegetation → Soil → Biodiversity
study_tips.tip_9: Use atlases extensively - Physical World Atlas, NCERT atlases, thematic maps
study_tips.tip_10: Group similar concepts - All landforms together, all wind patterns together for clarity

textbooks: NCERT Class 11 - Fundamentals of Physical Geography (Complete)
NCERT Class 11 - Practical Work in Geography Part I (Diagrams and Maps)
NCERT Class 12 - Fundamentals of Human Geography (Supplementary)
atlases: Nelles Map collection for specific regions
digital_tools: Google Earth - Virtual exploration of landforms and locations
Global Maps - Satellite imagery and terrain visualization
Climate and weather data from NOAA and WMO websites
supplementary: Mains answer writing practice (GS Paper I sample answers)
Magazine articles on current geographical issues
Documentary films on Earth science topics (BBC, Discovery, National Geographic)
